Facts.text: The Tower of Antonia is destroyed by the Romans
Sweden is defeated by Estonian tribes in the Battle of Lihula
Crete earthquake strikes with an estimated magnitude of 8, triggering a major tsunami that damages the Lighthouse of Alexandria and sweeps ships two miles inland in Egypt
Emperor Godaigo makes Sojiji the chief monastery of Soto Sect
Emperor Krishnadeva Raya is crowned, beginning the rejuvenation of the Vijayanagara Empire in southern India
Henry II of France declares war on England and orders the siege of Boulogne
Duke of Alva's army enters Brussels, Habsburg Netherlands (now Belgium)
Peace of Saint-Germain-en-Laye grants more freedom to Huguenots
John Davis enters Cumberland Sound in search of the Northwest Passage
The city of Oulu, Finland, is founded by Charles IX of Sweden
Dutch expedition of Joris van Spilbergen attacks and later pillages the Spanish-held town of Paita, north of Lima
The Irish Confederate Wars and Wars of the Three Kingdoms: English Parliamentary forces defeat Irish forces in the Battle of Dungan's Hill
City of Vilnius, capital of the Grand Duchy of Lithuania, falls to Muscovite forces, who pillage it with such ferocity that fires burn for two weeks
Dutch battle fleet of 23 ships demands the surrender of New York City
Vienna-based newspaper Wienerisches Diarium (later called Wiener Zeitung) prints its first edition and publishes daily for almost 320 years
First known ascent in a hot-air balloon by Bartolomeu de Gusmão (indoors)
Town of Baltimore is founded in the British North American colony of the Province of Maryland
British troops occupy and plunder Cherbourg
Jacques Balmat and Michel Paccard are the first to climb to the top of Mont Blanc, the highest mountain in Western Europe
The insurrection of Lyon occurs during the French Revolution
Joseph Whidbey and George Vancouver lead an expedition to search for the Northwest Passage near Juneau, Alaska
Boston African Society is established with 44 members
70 disciples of Gaon of Vilnius, arrive in Palestine
Peace negotiations begin in Ghent, United Netherlands (now Belgium)
Bavaria joins the Holy Alliance
French government of Polignac forms
The Stourbridge Lion steam locomotive, built by Foster, Rastrick and Co. in Stourbridge, England, goes into service for the Delaware and Hudson Canal Company in Honesdale, Pennsylvania
Battle of Hasselt: Dutch army drives out the Belgians
Beta Theta Pi is founded in Oxford, Ohio
Natal in South Africa becomes a British colony
Russian fleet appears at Nagasaki, Japan
Smith & Wesson patents metal bullet cartridges
Queen of the Sandwich Islands, Hawaii, arrives in New York City
American Civil War: Tennessee's "military" Governor Andrew Johnson frees his personal slaves, celebrated as a holiday by Tennessee African Americans in the early 20th century
Comet C/1864 N1 (Tempel) passes within 0.0964 AU (approximately 14.4 million km or 8.9 million miles) of Earth
Union troops and fleet occupy Fort Gaines, Alabama
Earthquake destroys the city of Arica, Chile

New York Yacht Club’s first defense of America's Cup: "Magic" (NYYC) defeats "Cambria" (Royal Thames YC, England; finishes 8th) and 16 fellow defenders from New York Harbor to Sandy Hook Light Vessel on the Atlantic and return (38 miles)
The Republic of Ploiești, a failed Radical-Liberal uprising against Domnitor Carol I of Romania
Dan O'Leary completes a 500-mile walk in 139 hours and 32 minutes
Snow falls on Lake Michigan
Riccardo Drigo's ballet "Le Réveil de Flore" premieres with the Imperial Mariinsky Ballet at the Peterhof Palace in St. Petersburg, Russian Empire
1st International Lawn Tennis Challenge (precursor to Davis Cup) begins at Longwood Cricket Club in Massachusetts, won 3-0 by the US over the British Isles
The US Army installs the first tricycle landing gear on the Army's Wright Flyer
Public Law 62-5 sets the number of representatives in the United States House of Representatives at 435 and comes into effect in 1913
The millionth patent is filed in the United States Patent Office by Francis Holton for a tubeless vehicle tire
Richard Corfield's "Camel Corps" opens against the "Mad Mullah" in Burao, Somalia
French troops under Gen. Bonneau occupy Mulhouse in Alsace
Montenegro declares war on Germany
The A's set an American League record with their 19th consecutive loss on the road
World War I: The Allies launch the Hundred Days Offensive, beginning with the Battle of Amiens, where 500 tanks and 10 Allied divisions attack German lines
Treaty of Rawalpindi: British recognize Afghanistan's independence
Tigers beat Yankees 1-0 in the shortest American League game, lasting 73 minutes
Pirates set a record of 46 hits in a doubleheader against the Phillies
British-Russian trade agreement is signed
First national march of the Ku Klux Klan (between 25,000 and 40,000 marchers) in Washington, D.C.
Salem airport in Oregon dedicated
MLB St. Louis Cardinals are 12 games back in the NL and go on to win the pennant
Washington Senators pitcher Bobby Burke no-hits Boston Red Sox, 5-0 at Griffith Stadium, Washington D. C.
A Finnish quinella in the 3,000 m steeplechase at the Berlin Olympics as Volmari Iso-Hollo beats teammate Kaarlo Tuominen in a world record time of 9:03.8
American Glenn Morris's decathlon total of 7,254 points is a world record; teammates Bob Clark and Jack Parker complete a US sweep in the grueling event at the Berlin Olympics
Corbett National Park and Tiger Reserve is founded in Uttarakhand, British India, as Hailey National Park (later renamed) as India's first national park
Bonneville Dam on the Columbia River begins producing power
Great Trek Centenary Celebrations commence; the Great Trek was a migration involving Boers leaving the Cape Colony and settling in the interior of South Africa
31 German aircraft are shot down over England
Twenty divisions of the Soviet 6th and 12th Armies surrender at Uman
British Flower class corvette HMS Dianthus sinks U-379
Six convicted Nazi saboteurs who land in the US are executed in Washington, D.C.
Soviet counteroffensive at Voronezh under Marshal Timoshenko
Common Chiefs of Staff meet in Quebec
US amphibians land at Sant'Agata on the north coast of Sicily
Vegetables and fruit are rationed in the Netherlands
Canadian and Polish troops occupy Cramesnil, Secqueville, Cintheaux, and St-Aignan
First test flight of the Junkers Ju 287, a proposed German Luftwaffe jet bomber; it is never fully put into production
US 15th Army corps occupies Le Mans
England and Australian armed services draw the fourth Victory Test
Soviet Union declares war on Japan during World War II and invades Manchuria the next day
Dreyfuss family, owners of MLB Pittsburgh Pirates since 1900, sells club to Frank McKinney and John Galbreath for $25 million
First flight of Convair B-36
India agrees to give Bhutan 32 square miles (82.88 square kilometers) of territory in the area of Dewangiri
Pakistan's national flag is approved
Bhutan, Land of the Dragon, becomes an independent monarchy
Syngman Rhee is re-elected as president of South Korea
US and South Korea initial a mutual security pact
Geneva Conference is held to discuss the peaceful uses of atomic energy
Fire and explosion kill 263 miners in Marcinelle, Belgium
WDIQ (now KMCT) TV channel 2 in Dozier, AL (PBS) begins broadcasting
USSR offers Syria economic and military aid
Brian Hyland's recording of "Itsy Bitsy Teenie Weenie Yellow Polkadot Bikini", a novelty pop song written by Paul Vance and Lee Pockriss, hits #1 on the Billboard Hot 100
Charges against 53 of the 76 Africans detained after the Sharpeville Massacre in South Africa are dropped
South Kasai secedes from the Congo
Hamilton Tiger-Cats (CFL) beat Buffalo Bills (NFL) 38-21 in Hamilton, Ontario
Verne Gagne beats Gene Kiniski in Minneapolis to become NWA champion
Great Train Robbery in England involves the theft of £2.6 million ($7.3 million)
The Kingsmen release "Louie, Louie;" some radio stations ban it due to rumors of obscene content
Verne Gagne beats Fritz Von Erich in Amarillo to become NWA Champion
First Rolling Stones concert in the Netherlands at the Kurhaus of Scheveningen in The Hague
The Dutch Opera forms in Amsterdam
Indonesia, Malaysia, the Philippines, Singapore, and Thailand meet to form the Association of Southeast Asian Nations (ASEAN)
Jarry Park is approved by Montreal Mayor Jean Drapeau for interim use by the Expos
Race riot in Miami, Florida
The Beatles are photographed by Iain MacMillan crossing the street as they walk away from EMI Recording Studios, for the cover of their "Abbey Road" album
A British soldier is shot dead by the Irish Republican Army in Belfast
France performs a nuclear test at Mururoa Atoll
New York Yankees sign a 30-year lease to play in remodeled Yankee Stadium
US PresidentRichard Nixonannounces he will resign at 12 p.m. the next day
As a result of rainfall from Typhoon Nina, the Banqiao Dam in China fails, causing the collapse of almost 6 million buildings and 229,000 deaths
Chicago White Sox wear shorts for a game instead of traditional baseball pants
Maurine Stuart is ordained as a Zen priest by Eido Tai Shimano Roshi
Texas Rangers turn their first triple play against the Athletics
Pioneer-Venus 2 with five atmospheric probes launches toward Venus
Lake Buena Vista Conference Center and Club Lake Villas open
At a summit, South African trade unions resolve to defy apartheid and labor laws
Jury in KC, Mo, awards TV anchorwoman Christine Craft $500,000 in a sex discrimination suit against KMBC-TV (later overturned)
Milwaukee's 10-game losing streak ends
Nawal El Moutawakel of Morocco becomes the first female Olympic champion from a Muslim nation and the first from her country in the 400 m hurdles at the Los Angeles Olympics
Baseball's new agreement permits two new National League teams in 1993
Japan launches Planet-A, a probe to Halley's Comet
Altaf Hussain addresses Nishtar Park in Karachi, announcing the establishment of the Muttahida Qaumi Movement (MQM) political movement

Texas paroles American rock singer David Crosby from state prison five months into his five-year prison sentence for drug and weapons convictions; he credits the reprieve with helping him break his cocaine addiction
Brewers' Rob Deer strikes out five times in a game
Pakistan is all out for 708 against England at The Oval, with Botham taking 3-217
Angola, Cuba and South Africa agree to a ceasefire in the Angolan Civil War
Army in Rangoon, Myanmar fires on pro-democracy students, killing thousands during the "8888 Uprising"
Astronomers announce the discovery of the most distant galaxy known, 4C41.17, a cluster of stars more than 10 billion light-years away
Ceasefire between Iran and Iraq takes effect after eight years of war
Jennifer Levin's parents file a $25 million lawsuit against Dorrian's Red Hand Bar
Minnesota Twins pull second triple play of year in 6-2 win over Cleveland
Renovated Central Park Zoo reopens after 4 years in New York City
Russian troops begin to pull out of Afghanistan after 9 years of war
Sarah, Duchess of York, gives birth to her first child, Beatrice, weighing 6 lb 12 oz (3.06 kg)
Temperature hits a high of 88°F on 8/8/88 in NYC
The Cubs and Phillies attempt to play the first night game ever at Wrigley Field but are rained out in the fourth inning with Chicago leading 3-1
US space shuttle STS-28 is launched
Baltimore Orioles pull off their 10th triple play (1-6-3 vs. Oakland)
Carlton Fisk tiesJohnny Benchby hitting 327 home runs as a catcher
Iraq annexes Kuwait as its 19th province
Shiite Muslims release British hostage John McCarthy
The Warsaw radio mast, at one time the tallest construction ever built, collapses
With two months left in the season, the White Sox set a new home attendance record of 2,150,951 attendees
Bulgarian canoeist Nikolay Bukhalov completes the C-1 500/1,000 double, winning the latter at the Barcelona Olympics
Fermín Cacho wins Spain's first Olympic gold medal in a running event when he takes the 1,500 m at the Barcelona Games
Germany goes through the field hockey competition undefeated to win gold with a 2-1 victory over Australia in the men's final at the Barcelona Olympics
Lou Whitaker hits his 200th career home run
Spanish center forward Kiko scores twice as the home team wins the Olympic football gold medal with a 3-2 victory over Poland in front of 95,000 at Camp Nou, Barcelona
Tropical Storm Bret ravages Venezuela, killing about 100 people
10.75 inches (27.305 cm) of rainfall at Lockington Dam, Lockington, Ohio (state record)
Jeff King of the Pirates is the 16th National League player to hit two home runs in one inning
American jazz artist Mel Tormé (70) suffers a stroke, ending his 65-year singing career
Mariner Randy Johnson strikes out 19 Chicago White Sox players
The UN approves a sale-price formula for Iraqi crude oil sales under the oil-for-food plan
Confederate submarine CSS H.L. Hunley is raised to the surface after 136 years on the ocean floor off the coast of South Carolina
An EF2 tornado touches down in Kings County and Richmond County, New York State, the most powerful tornado in New York to date and the first in Brooklyn since 1889
Russia launches a full-scale land, air, and sea invasion of Georgia, referring to it as a "peace enforcement" operation after Georgia responds to aggression from South Ossetia
XXIX Summer Olympic Games open in Beijing, China
China announces plans to close 23 rare earth mines and up to 50 smelting companies
Flooding due to heavy rain in Manila, Philippines, kills 16 people
14 people are killed by a bomb explosion in Nangarhar, Afghanistan
SpaniardJuan Ponce de Leónfounds Caparra, the first European settlement in Puerto Rico
Cornerstone is laid forTycho Brahe's observatory in Uraniborg, Denmark, which, when built, becomes the world's most advanced research institution
Pope Sixtus excommunicates Henry of Navarre (Henry IV)
Galileo Galileipresents his telescope to the Venetian Senate in Venice
French KingLouis XVIconvenes the Estates-General, held in May 1789
Brigham Youngis chosen as the head of the Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints following the death of Joseph Smith
Thomas Edisonis granted the patent for autographic printing (US Patent 180,857)
Ernest Shackleton's ship "Endurance" leaves Plymouth, England, on the final major expedition of the Heroic Age of Antarctic Exploration
7th Venice Film Festival opens with a United States boycott due toBenito Mussolini's Fascist Italian regime
GeneralBernard Montgomeryis appointed commander of the British 8th Army at El Alamein
Florence Chadwickswims across the English Channel in a record time of 13 hours and 23 minutes
Soviet leaderGeorgy Malenkovreports possession of a hydrogen bomb
Fidel Castroforms the "26th of July Movement," a Cuban vanguard revolutionary organization
South African Broadcasting bans the playing of all Beatles songs due toJohn Lennon's remark that the band is "more popular than Jesus"
Republican Convention in Miami Beach nominatesRichard Nixonfor President
MLB New York Yankees honorCasey Stengelby retiring his jersey number 37; Stengel manages the team from 1949 to 1960, winning 7 World Series titles in 10 appearances
US Vice PresidentSpiro Agnewsays reports he takes kickbacks from government contracts in Maryland are "damned lies" and vows not to resign
Hank Williams Jr. falls off the side of Ajax Peak in Montana
Iraqi PresidentSaddam Husseinexecutes 22 political opponents
Columbia Records releases "An Innocent Man," singer-songwriterBilly Joel's ninth studio album; it features three Billboard Top 10 hit singles: "Tell Her About It," "Uptown Girl," and the title track
Military coup in Guatemala; dictator and former GeneralEfraín Ríos Monttflees
Carl Lewiswins his third gold medal of the Los Angeles Olympics, joining Kirk Baptiste and Thomas Jefferson in an American sweep of the 200 m
Future five-time America's Cup winnerRussell Couttsof New Zealand wins the Finn Class sailing gold medal at the Los Angeles Olympics
Rap group N.W.A releases their debut studio album "Straight Outta Compton"
US Secretary of StateGeorge P. Shultznarrowly escapes an assassination attempt in Bolivia
New Zealand Prime MinisterDavid Langeresigns after five years due to a no-confidence issue
Former MLB baseball player and managerPete Rosebegins a 5-month prison term at Marion (IL) Federal Prison Camp for filing false income tax returns
ActorGary Oldmanis arrested for drunk driving
Rock guitar heroCarlos Santanapleads no contest to marijuana possession charge in Houston, Texas
Singer-keyboardistBilly Prestonis charged with exhibiting pornography to a minor
Metallicasinger and guitaristJames Hetfieldsuffers second and third-degree burns during a pyrotechnics explosion on stage at Olympic Stadium in Montreal

The original US "Dream Team" wins the basketball gold at the Barcelona Olympics 117-85 over Croatia, featuring superstarsMichael Jordan,Magic Johnson,Larry Bird,Scottie Pippen,Charles Barkley, andPatrick Ewing
"Gangster's Paradise" single released byCoolio(Billboard Song of the Year, 1995)
John Elwayis inducted into the Pro Football Hall of Fame
ActorSylvester Stalloneand former heavyweight boxing contender Chuck Wepner settle a lawsuit out of court for an undisclosed sum; Wepner claims he is the inspiration for the "Rocky" movies
Joe Liebermanloses the Democratic Senate primary in Connecticut to Ned Lamont and announces he will run as an independent candidate
Carlos SlimovertakesBill Gatesas the world's richest person with an estimated net worth of US$59 billion, according to "Fortune"
US Congress unanimously chooses the dollar as the monetary unit for the United States of America
German airshipGraf Zeppelinbegins the first round-the-world flight by airship, departing from Lakehurst, New Jersey
The German "Aufbau Ost" directive is signed byWilhelm Keitelto mobilize German forces in the East before the start of Operation Barbarossa and the invasion of the Soviet Union
PresidentHarry Trumansigns the United Nations Charter
US, USSR, Britain, and France sign the Treaty of London, which sets down procedures for the Nuremberg war trials of Nazi leaders
Horikawa, 73rd Emperor of Japan (1087-1107), born in Japan (d. 1107)
Saint Dominic, Castilian Catholic priest, founder of the Dominican Order, and patron saint of astronomers, born in Caleruega, Spain (d. 1221)
Conrad Lycosthenes, French-born German humanist and encyclopedist, born in Rouffach, France (d. 1561)
John Ward, English composer, born in Canterbury, England (d. 1638)
Adolf, Count of Nassau-Siegen, third son of Count ruling Count John VII, born at Dillenburg Castle, Dillenburg (d. 1608)
Cecilius Calvert, 2nd Baron Baltimore, British peer granted charter to settle Maryland, born in Kent, England (d. 1675)
Joris van Geel, Belgian missionary in Africa (earliest know dictionary of Congolese Bantu), born in Oevel, Spanish Netherlands (d. 1652)[1]
Godfrey Kneller, German-British portrait and court painter, born in Lübeck  (d. 1723)
Jacques Basnage, French historian and vicar (History of the Reformed Churches), born in Rouen, France (d. 1723)
John Ker, Scottish spy who spied for the British government against the Jacobites, born in Crawfurdland, Scotland (d. 1726)
Laurent Belissen, French Baroque composer, born in Aix-en-Provence, France (d. 1762)
Francis Hutcheson, Irish-Scots philosopher during Scottish Enlightenment, born in Drumalig, Ireland (d. 1746)
Gustaaf Willem, baron van Imhoff, Dutch statesman (Governor of Ceylon 1736-40, Governor of the Dutch East Indies 1743-50), born in Leer, Netherlands (d. 1750)
Tokugawa Ietsugu, 7th Japanese shōgun of the Tokugawa shogunate (1713-6), born in Edo, Japan (d. 1716)
Carl Fredrik Pechlin, Swedish politician who is thought to have organized assassination of King Gustav III, born in Holstein, Germany (d. 1796)
Johann Christoph Adelung, German linguist, librarian, translator and lexicographer, born in Spantekow, Duchy of Mecklenburg-Strelitz (now Germany) (d. 1806)
Hieronymus van Alphen, Dutch poet of children's poems (Proeve van kleine gedigen voor Kinder), born in Gouda, The Netherlands (d. 1803)
Luigi Marchesi, Italian castrato singer, born in Milan, Habsburg Empire (d. 1829)
Charles Bulfinch, 1st American professional architect (Massachusetts State House), born in Boston, Massachusetts (d. 1844)
Antoon Jurgens, Dutch butter and margarine manufacturer, born in Oss, Netherlands (d. 1880)
Emilie Flygare-Carlén, Swedish novelist, most read novelist of her time, born in Strömstad, Sweden (d. 1892)
John Rodgers II, American admiral, commander in the Union Navy, born near Havre de Grace, Maryland (d. 1882)
Esther Morris [Esther Hobart McQuigg], American suffragist, anti-slavery activist, and 1st female justice of the peace in the US, born in Tioga County, New York (d. 1902)
Charles Anderson Dana, American newspaper editor and Assistant Secretary of War for Union during Civil War, born in Hinsdale, New Hampshire (d. 1897)
Winfield Scott Featherston "Old Swet", American politician and Army Brigadier General (Confederate), born near Murfreesboro, Tennessee (d. 1891)
Théodule-Augustin Ribot, French realist painter (Breton Fishermen and Their Families), born in Saint-Nicolas-d'Attez, France (d. 1891)
Maria Alexandrovna (Marie of Hesse), German-born Tsarina of Russia as first wife of Emperor Alexander II, born in Darmstadt, Duchy of Hesse (d. 1880)
James William Forsyth, American Brigadier General (Union Army), born in Maumee, Ohio (d. 1906)
Nelson Appleton Miles, American Major General (Union Army), born in Westminster, Massachusetts (d. 1925)
Barend Barendse, Dutch actor and director (Heilig recht), born in Amsterdam, Noord-Holland, Netherlands (d. 1935)
Cécile Chaminade, French pianist and 1st female composer awarded with Chevalier de la Légion d'Honneur (1913), born in Paris, France (d. 1944)
Henry Fairfield Osborn, American paleontologist, geologist (founded US Eugenics Society), born in Fairfield, Connecticut (d. 1935)
William Bateson, English biologist (originator of term "genetics"), born in Whitby, England (d. 1926)
Henri de Vries [Hendricus Van Walterop], Dutch actor (Cleopatra, White Cargo), born in Rotterdam, Zuid-Holland, Netherlands (d. 1949)
Matthew Henson, American explorer, 1st African American to reach North Pole (Jun 4 1909), born in Nanjemoy, Maryland (d. 1955)
William Henry Squire, British cellist, music educator, and composer, born in Ross-on-Wye, Herefordshire, England (d. 1963)
Artur Bernardes, President of Brazil (1922-26) founder of the Republican Party, born in Santa Rita do Turvo, Brazil (d. 1955)
"Dr. Bob" Smith, American physician and co-founder of Alcoholics Anonymous, born in St. Johnsbury, Vermont (d. 1950)
Earle Page, Australian politician, 11th Prime Minister of Australia (1939), born in Grafton, New South Wales (d. 1961)
Paul Ludwig Ewald von Kleist, German general (led the "blitzkrieg", 1st Panzer Group on Eastern Front), born in Braunfels, Germany Empire (d. 1954)
Ladislas Starevich, Polish animator, created stop-motion animation ("The Cameraman’s Revenge"), born in Moscow, Russia (d. 1965)
Sara Teasdale, American lyric poet (Pulitzer Prize 1918 "Love Songs"), born in St Louis, Missouri (d. 1933)
Daniel Ruyneman, Dutch composer and pianist, born in Amsterdam, Netherlands (d. 1963)

Pietro Yon, Italian-American organist (St. Patrick's Cathedral, 1927-43; St. Francis Xavier Church, 1907-26), composer (Gesù bambino; Concerto Gregoriano), and educator, born in Settimo Vittone, Italy (d. 1943)[1]
Esme Percy, British actor (Murder!, Pygmalion, Old Spanish Custom), born in London, England (d. 1957)
Malcolm Keen, British actor (The Lodger, The Manxman, The Murder Party), born in Bristol, England (d. 1970)
Ullrich Haupt, Prussian actor (Morocco, Du Barry-Woman of Passion), born in Falkenburg, Pomerania, Germany (d. 1931)
Jack Ryder, Australian cricket all-rounder (20 Tests, 1,394 runs @ 51.62), born in Collingwood, Victoria (d. 1977)
Adolf Busch, German-Swiss violinist (Busch Quartet, 1920-51), conductor (Lucerne Festival; Marlboro Festival), and composer, born in Siegen, Germany (d. 1952)
Marjorie Kinnan Rawlings, American writer (The Yearling), born in Washington, D.C. (d. 1953)
Alexis Minotis [Alexandros Minotakis], Greek actor (The Merchant of Venice, King Lear), born in Chania, Crete, Greece (d. 1990)
Victor Young, American violinist, arranger, orchestra leader, and composer ("Street of Dreams"; "Love Letters"; "I Don't Stand a Ghost of a Chance with You"), born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 1956)
Josephine Holt Bay, American 1st woman to head a firm on NY stock exchange, born in Iowa (d. 1962)
Ernest Lawrence, American nuclear scientist and inventor (Cyclotron - Nobel 1939), born in Canton, South Dakota (d. 1958)
Dayton Lummis, American character actor (The Court-Martial of Billy Mitchell; The Wrong Man; Moonfire), born in Summit, New Jersey (d. 1988)
(William) Elmo Tanner, American big band singer, whistler (Ted Weems Orchestra - "Heartaches"), bandleader, and DJ, born in Nashville, Tennessee (d. 1990)
Achille Varzi, Italian auto racer (14 Grand Prix wins), born in Galliate, Italy (d. 1948)
István Szelényi, Hungarian pianist and composer, born in Altsohl, Austria-Hungary (d. 1972)
André Jolivet, French composer (Jeune France; Mana), born in Paris, France (d. 1974)
Nino Martini, Italian actor (The Gay Desperado, Here's to Romance), born in Verona, Veneto, Italy (d. 1976)
André Demedts, Belgian Flemish writer (Neerslag der dagen (Depression of the Days)), born in Wielsbeke, Belgian (d. 1992)
(Bennett) "Benny" Carter, American Grammy Award-winning jazz saxophonist, clarinetist, trumpeter, arranger, composer ("Harlem Renaissance Suite"), and bandleader, born in New York City (d. 2003)
Arthur Goldberg, American jurist and statesman (UN ambassador, Supreme Court justice 1962-65), born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 1990)
Chivu Stoica, Romanian politician (Premier of Romania 1955-61), born in Smeeni, Romania (d. 1975)
Emilio Flores Márquez "Don Millo", Puerto Rican sugar cane worker and centenarian, (world's oldest man in 2021), born in Carolina, Pueto Rico (d. 2021)[1]
Bill Voce, English cricket fast bowler (27 Tests, 98 wickets, BB 7/70; Nottinghamshire CCC), born in Annesley Woodhouse, England (d. 1984)
Francisco Brochado Da Rocha, Brazilian professor and politician (Prime Minister of Brazil 1962), born in Porte Alegre, Brazil (d. 1962)

Lucky Millinder [Lucius Venable], American swing and rhythm-and-blues bandleader (Rosetta Tharpe - "Shout, Sister, Shout"; Wynonie Harris - "Who Threw The Whiskey in the Well"), born in Anniston, Alabama (d. 1966)
Sylvia Sidney [Sophia Kosow], American actress (Sabotage, Beetlejuice, Demon), born in The Bronx, New York (d. 1999)
Rosetta LeNoire, American actress (Family Matters, The Brother from Another Planet), born in New York City (d. 2002)
Daniel Mann [Chugerman], American stage and screen director (The Rose Tattoo; The Teahouse Of The August Moon; Butterfield 8), born in Brooklyn, New York (d. 1991)
Axel Stordahl, American arranger (Tommy Dorsey; Frank Sinatra), orchestra leader (Eddie Fisher), and composer (McHales's Navy theme), born in Staten Island, New York (d. 1963)
Cecil Travis, American baseball infielder (1st player to get 5 hits on debut; MLB All Star 1938, 40, 41; Washington Senators), born in Riverdale, Georgia (d. 2006)
Daan Wildschut, Dutch painter, and sculptor (stained glass works), born in Grave, Netherlands (d. 1995)
Robert Stafford, American politician (Sen-R-VT, 1971-88), born in Rutland, Vermont (d. 2006)
(C. Dudley) "Pete" King, American film and easy listening music arranger and composer (The Pied Piper of Hamelin; The Last of the Secret Agents?), born in Greenville, Ohio (d. 1982)
Unity Mitford, English fascist sympathizer and one of the Mitford sisters, born in London (d. 1948)
Jumbo Elliott, American track coach of Villanova University Wildcats, coached 5 Olympic Gold medalists, born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania (d. 1981)
Dino De Laurentiis, Italian film producer (King Kong), born in Torre Annunziata, Italy (d. 2010)
Don Cook, American foreign Correspondent (The New York Herald Tribune), born in Bridgeport, Connecticut (d. 1995)
Jimmy Witherspoon, American jump blues singer ("Ain't Nobody's Business"), born in Gurdon, Arkansas (d. 1997)
Leo Chiosso, Italian lyricist (Fred Buscaglione), and screenwriter, born in Chieri, Italy (d. 2006)
Esther Williams, American swimmer and actress (Dangerous When Wet), born in Inglewood California (d. 2013)
Roger Nixon, American composer, born in Tulare, California (d. 2009)
Vulimiri Ramalingaswami, Indian medical scientist, born in Srikakulam, British India (d. 2001)
Webb Pierce, American honky-tonk country singer-songwriter, and guitarist ("In The Jailhouse Now"; "Wondering"; "There Stands The Glass"), born in West Monroe, Louisiana (d. 1991)
Gertrude Himmelfarb, American conservative historian, born in New York City (d. 2019)
Rory Calhoun [Francis Timothy McCown], American actor (Blue & Gray, How to Marry a Millionaire), born in Los Angeles, California (d. 1999)
Rudi Gernreich, Austrian-American fashion designer (monokini - 1st women's topless swimsuit), born in Vienna, Austria (d. 1985)
Gene Deitch, American illustrator (Tom Terrific, Nudnik), born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 2020)
Thomas Beversdorf, American classical composer ("Of Walruses, Cheesecake, and Morse Code"), and educator (Indiana University, 1949-80), born in Yoakum, Texas (d. 1981)
Alija Izetbegović, Bosnian politician and 1st president of the Republic of Bosnia and Herzegovina (1990-2000), born in Bosanski Šamac, Kingdom of Serbs, Croats and Slovenes (d. 2003)
Ferdinando Orlandi, Italian actor (The House with Laughing Windows), born in Italy (d. 1991)
Richard Anderson, American actor (Oscar Goldman- The 6 Million Dollar Man, The Bionic Woman television series), born in Long Branch, New Jersey (d. 2017)
Urban "Urbie" Green, American jazz trombonist (Woody Herman; Buck Clayton; Hugo Montenegro), born in Mobile, Alabama (d. 2018)
Elis Juliana, Curaçao writer and poet (Lady of the Night: A flower), born in Curaçao (d. 2013)
Jim Weaver, American politician (US Representatives from Oregon, 1975-87), businessman, and WWII veteran, born in Brookings, South Dakota (d. 2020)

Yuri Kazakov, Russian writer (Arcturus the Hunting Hound; Autumn in the Oak Woods), born in Moscow, Russa (d. 1982)
